stage.name: Structural hazard: when the value is ready but the resource is not
stage.description: A structural-hazard lesson on functional-unit readiness, result-bus sharing, unknown-latency operations, multiplier timing, LSU readiness, and in-order blocking.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: A ready operand is not enough

content.title: Structural hazard: when the value is ready but the resource is not
content.subtitle: Structural hazards ask whether the functional unit and result path can actually accept the instruction now.
content.thesis: Data readiness answers only one question. Structural readiness asks whether the backend resources can safely carry the work.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: structural hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Issue checks functional-unit and result-bus readiness; unknown-latency integer division and selected CSR behavior can block shared result-bus users, multiplication has a fixed two-cycle latency with specific blocking behavior, and LSU issue waits for LSU readiness.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson separates data hazards from structural hazards. A data hazard asks whether the operand value is safe. A structural hazard asks whether the execution resource and result path are available. In CV32A60X, Issue_read_operands checks functional-unit readiness and result-bus conflicts before sending work into EX_STAGE.

actions.text: This lesson separates data hazards from structural hazards. A data hazard asks whether the operand value is safe. A structural hazard asks whether the execution resource and result path are available. In CV32A60X, Issue_read_operands checks functional-unit readiness and result-bus conflicts before sending work into EX_STAGE.

scenes.title: Structural hazard means FU plus RB

content.title: The functional unit and result bus both matter
content.subtitle: A unit may not be ready, or the path used to return a result may be occupied.

columns.title: Functional unit
columns.body: ISSUE_STAGE checks whether the target FU is ready to execute this instruction.

columns.title: Result bus
columns.body: Some functional units share a result-bus path, so a long or recent operation can block others.

content.takeaway: Structural readiness is about where the instruction will execute and how its result will come back.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: structural hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Issue checks functional-unit and result-bus readiness; unknown-latency integer division and selected CSR behavior can block shared result-bus users, multiplication has a fixed two-cycle latency with specific blocking behavior, and LSU issue waits for LSU readiness.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: EX_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: EX_STAGE encapsulates functional units including ALU, Branch Unit, CSR buffer, Mult, load/store, and CVXIF; it receives issued work and returns results to ISSUE_STAGE.

content.speakerNotes: Structural hazards are resource questions. The first resource is the functional unit: can this unit accept work now? The second is the result path: can the result come back without colliding with another user? That is why the source talks about both FU readiness and result-bus readiness. Backend correctness lives in both places.

actions.text: Structural hazards are resource questions. The first resource is the functional unit: can this unit accept work now? The second is the result path: can the result come back without colliding with another user? That is why the source talks about both FU readiness and result-bus readiness. Backend correctness lives in both places.

content.title: Division and selected CSR behavior need conservative blocking
content.subtitle: When EX_STAGE reports that an unknown-latency operation is running, shared result-bus users must wait.

steps.title: Long operation running
steps.body: Integer division and some CSR behavior can have unknown latency.

steps.title: Shared result bus risk
steps.body: ALU, BRANCH, CSR, and MULT can share a result-bus conflict domain.

steps.title: Issue blocks
steps.body: Instructions using those paths are blocked while the unknown-latency user is active.

steps.title: Avoid collision
steps.body: The backend protects the returned result path from ambiguous ownership.

content.takeaway: A structural stall can be caused by future writeback conflict, not just by a busy execution box.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: structural hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Issue checks functional-unit and result-bus readiness; unknown-latency integer division and selected CSR behavior can block shared result-bus users, multiplication has a fixed two-cycle latency with specific blocking behavior, and LSU issue waits for LSU readiness.

content.speakerNotes: Unknown latency is where conservative control makes sense. If integer division or certain CSR behavior is running with unknown latency, EX_STAGE reports that condition. Instructions using ALU, branch, CSR, or multiply paths can then be blocked to avoid conflicts on a shared result bus. The key teaching point is that the hazard is about future result return, not only about starting execution.

actions.text: Unknown latency is where conservative control makes sense. If integer division or certain CSR behavior is running with unknown latency, EX_STAGE reports that condition. Instructions using ALU, branch, CSR, or multiply paths can then be blocked to avoid conflicts on a shared result bus. The key teaching point is that the hazard is about future result return, not only about starting execution.

scenes.title: Multiplier and LSU have different rules

content.title: Do not collapse all structural stalls into one sentence
content.subtitle: The source gives distinct rules for multiplication and LSU readiness.

cards.title: MULT fixed latency
cards.body: Multiplications have a fixed latency of two cycles.

cards.title: One-cycle block
cards.body: ALU, BRANCH, and CSR users block if MULT issued one cycle earlier.

cards.title: MULT pipelined
cards.body: Instructions using MULT are not blocked because the multiplier can accept one per cycle.

cards.title: LSU readiness
cards.body: Instructions using LSU are blocked if LSU is not ready.

content.takeaway: Structural-hazard rules are resource-specific; generic wording loses the design.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: structural hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Issue checks functional-unit and result-bus readiness; unknown-latency integer division and selected CSR behavior can block shared result-bus users, multiplication has a fixed two-cycle latency with specific blocking behavior, and LSU issue waits for LSU readiness.

content.speakerNotes: The multiplier and LSU examples show why generic wording is weak. Multiplications have fixed two-cycle latency. ALU, branch, and CSR instructions block if a multiply issued one cycle earlier, to avoid result-bus conflict. But multiply instructions themselves are not blocked because the multiplier is pipelined and can accept one per cycle. LSU is simpler to state: LSU users block if LSU is not ready.

actions.text: The multiplier and LSU examples show why generic wording is weak. Multiplications have fixed two-cycle latency. ALU, branch, and CSR instructions block if a multiply issued one cycle earlier, to avoid result-bus conflict. But multiply instructions themselves are not blocked because the multiplier is pipelined and can accept one per cycle. LSU is simpler to state: LSU users block if LSU is not ready.

scenes.title: Debug structural stalls in this order

content.title: When operands look ready but issue still stalls
content.subtitle: Use a structural-hazard checklist before blaming RAW or WAW.

items.title: Check target FU
items.body: Is the needed functional unit ready?

items.title: Check shared result bus
items.body: Is an unknown-latency DIV or CSR-style operation occupying the return path?

items.title: Check recent MULT
items.body: Did a multiply issue one cycle earlier and block ALU/BRANCH/CSR users?

items.title: Check LSU ready
items.body: Load/store instructions wait if LSU is not ready.

items.title: Remember in-order issue
items.body: A structural stall in the older instruction blocks younger candidates.

content.takeaway: A structural hazard is a resource explanation for a stall that data readiness cannot explain.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: structural hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Issue checks functional-unit and result-bus readiness; unknown-latency integer division and selected CSR behavior can block shared result-bus users, multiplication has a fixed two-cycle latency with specific blocking behavior, and LSU issue waits for LSU readiness.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: Issue_read_operands submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Issue_read_operands tracks hazards, obtains input operands, uses register file values by default, can replace operands with forwarded values, and sends issued instructions to EX_STAGE through a register.

content.speakerNotes: Here is the practical debugging order. If operands look ready but issue stalls, check the target functional unit. Then check shared result-bus conflicts from unknown-latency operations. Then check whether a multiply issued one cycle earlier. Then check LSU readiness. Finally remember the in-order rule: if the older instruction is structurally blocked, younger work waits too.

actions.text: Here is the practical debugging order. If operands look ready but issue stalls, check the target functional unit. Then check shared result-bus conflicts from unknown-latency operations. Then check whether a multiply issued one cycle earlier. Then check LSU readiness. Finally remember the in-order rule: if the older instruction is structurally blocked, younger work waits too.

questions.question: What does a structural hazard primarily check in this lesson?

options.label: Whether the required functional unit and result path are ready
Whether the branch direction counter is strongly taken
Whether the instruction came from GitHub

questions.analysis: Structural hazards are resource-readiness checks involving functional units and result buses.

questions.question: What happens when LSU is not ready for an LSU instruction?
questions.analysis: The source states that instructions using LSU are blocked if LSU is not ready.

questions.question: Which statements about multiplication structural behavior are source-backed?

options.label: Multiplications have fixed latency of two cycles
ALU, BRANCH, and CSR users can be blocked if MULT issued one cycle earlier
MULT users are not blocked because the multiplier is pipelined
MULT requires a BTB target address

questions.analysis: The first three statements are in the structural-hazard description; BTB has no role in multiplier issue.

questions.question: Why can unknown-latency division or CSR behavior block ALU, BRANCH, CSR, or MULT users?

options.label: To avoid conflicts on a shared result bus
To force all branches to be not taken
To reset the frontend PC after every instruction
To make the scoreboard capacity unlimited

questions.analysis: The source connects unknown-latency operations to blocking shared result-bus users.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: structural hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Issue checks functional-unit and result-bus readiness; unknown-latency integer division and selected CSR behavior can block shared result-bus users, multiplication has a fixed two-cycle latency with specific blocking behavior, and LSU issue waits for LSU readiness.

content.speakerNotes: This checkpoint completes the five-lesson Unit 05 batch. You should now separate data hazards from structural hazards, and then separate different structural cases from each other. That may feel fussy, but fussiness is often just accuracy wearing a work shirt.

actions.text: This checkpoint completes the five-lesson Unit 05 batch. You should now separate data hazards from structural hazards, and then separate different structural cases from each other. That may feel fussy, but fussiness is often just accuracy wearing a work shirt.

scenes.title: Unit 05 now has its hazard spine

content.title: Issue readiness means data safe plus resource ready
content.subtitle: The issue stage now reads as a set of grounded permission checks instead of a vague dispatch box.
takeaways: Data hazards ask whether operands and destinations are safe.
Forwarding can satisfy some RAW hazards with legal ready values.
Structural hazards ask whether FUs and result paths are ready.
content.nextStep: Next recommended lesson: Checkpoint 05, then Unit 06 on execute units, branch resolution, commit, and precise exceptions.
content.closing: At this point, CVA6 backend reading has a grammar: entries, hazards, forwarding, resources, and ordered commit.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ISSUE_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ISSUE_STAGE issues instructions in-order, reorders execution results, and sends completed instructions in-order to COMMIT_STAGE.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: structural hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Issue checks functional-unit and result-bus readiness; unknown-latency integer division and selected CSR behavior can block shared result-bus users, multiplication has a fixed two-cycle latency with specific blocking behavior, and LSU issue waits for LSU readiness.

content.speakerNotes: You now have the Unit 05 hazard spine. Issue readiness means the data must be safe and the resource must be ready. Forwarding can satisfy some RAW cases, but structural hazards are a separate resource story. The natural next step is a checkpoint, then Unit 06: execute units, branch resolution, commit, and precise exceptions.
